title: The Friends Of The Fallen fortunes
content:
혻혻혻혻The battlefield behind us,
혻혻혻혻And night loomed on the track;
혻혻혻혻The Friends of Fallen Fortunes
혻혻혻혻Were riding at my back.
혻혻혻혻Save those who lay face upward
혻혻혻혻Upon the sodden plain,
혻혻혻혻Not one of all I占폻 trusted
혻혻혻혻Was missing from my train.

혻혻혻혻A draggled train and blood-stained,
혻혻혻혻With helmets dented in,
혻혻혻혻With battered, loosened armour,
혻혻혻혻But with a cheerful grin.
혻혻혻혻No dark look bent upon me;
혻혻혻혻I noted to my shame
혻혻혻혻That Friends of Fallen Fortunes
혻혻혻혻Are aye the last to blame.


혻혻혻혻Not one of all I占폻 trusted,
혻혻혻혻Who占폻 followed to their cost,
혻혻혻혻Save those who lay face upward
혻혻혻혻On that red field I占폻 lost;
혻혻혻혻And here and there a soldier
혻혻혻혻I占폻 trusted not at all,
혻혻혻혻Like an unexpected mourner
혻혻혻혻At a poor man占퐏 funeral.

혻혻혻혻And as the horses stumbled,
혻혻혻혻And the footmen limped along,
혻혻혻혻They all joined in the chorus
혻혻혻혻Of a good old Next Time song.
혻혻혻혻Behind us in the distance,
혻혻혻혻By hill and lane and wood,
혻혻혻혻My ever-dwindling rear-guard
혻혻혻혻Fell back again and stood.

혻혻혻혻They recked not wounds nor losses,
혻혻혻혻They all seemed very kind,
혻혻혻혻From knight who rode beside me
혻혻혻혻To boor who limped behind;
혻혻혻혻And some borne in their litters
혻혻혻혻Through that long agony,
혻혻혻혻Their death-white, pain-drawn faces
혻혻혻혻Had no reproach for me.

혻혻혻혻And so from noon till darkness,
혻혻혻혻Till morning grim and grey,
혻혻혻혻The Earl占퐏 son and the Peasant占퐏
혻혻혻혻Were brothers that dark day.
혻혻혻혻I straightened in my saddle,
혻혻혻혻And proudly glanced me round,
혻혻혻혻I still was King of Comrades,
혻혻혻혻Whoever might be crowned!

혻혻혻혻I straightened in my saddle,
혻혻혻혻And glanced round proudly then,
혻혻혻혻Whoe占폾r might reign a season,
혻혻혻혻I held the hearts of men!
혻혻혻혻No power of gold can buy them
혻혻혻혻While battles shall be fought,
혻혻혻혻The Friends of Fallen Fortunes
혻혻혻혻Are never to be bought.

혻혻혻혻Through rain and marsh and hunger,
혻혻혻혻To what their fate might bring,
혻혻혻혻The remnants of my legions
혻혻혻혻Toiled on to join their King.
혻혻혻혻From north and south the captains
혻혻혻혻Of scattered bands won through,
혻혻혻혻Beneath its beaten colours
혻혻혻혻My beaten army grew.

혻혻혻혻And in the West before us,
혻혻혻혻The West was ever thus,
혻혻혻혻More Friends of Fallen Fortunes
혻혻혻혻Were gathering food for us;
혻혻혻혻For refuge and for succour,
혻혻혻혻For safety, food and rest,
혻혻혻혻The best of beaten armies
혻혻혻혻For ever seek the West.


혻혻혻혻With these men for my captains,
혻혻혻혻When we marched east again,
혻혻혻혻Our enemies were scattered
혻혻혻혻Like dust across the plain.
혻혻혻혻Our city lay before us,
혻혻혻혻And as we marched along,
혻혻혻혻We joined the grand old chorus
혻혻혻혻Of the glorious Next Time song.
혻혻혻혻And though they wear no armour,
혻혻혻혻And bear no blade nor bill,
혻혻혻혻The Friends of Fallen Fortunes
혻혻혻혻Are riding with me still;
혻혻혻혻And, many times defeated
혻혻혻혻By city, field, and sea,
혻혻혻혻The Friends of Fallen Fortunes
혻혻혻혻March on to Victory.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
